{"step_by_step_thinking": "The query asks whether salivary cortisol can be used to evaluate pain in neonates. Reviewing the documents, Document [0] mentions that scoring tools with objective parameters are available for neonates, including salivary cortisol level, pupillary size, serum levels of BDNF, and skin conductance. It states that such tools have'some clinical significance, but their application in day-to-day management is subject to limitations by practical difficulties and limited data that validate their significance.' This implies that while salivary cortisol is one of the parameters considered, its use for evaluating pain is not well-established or validated. Document [13] further notes that salivary cortisol can correlate well with free cortisol levels and is easily obtainable, but data for interpretation in critically ill patients (including pain assessment) is very limited. There is no direct evidence or recommendation in the documents that salivary cortisol is used specifically or reliably to evaluate pain in neonates. Therefore, based on the available information, salivary cortisol is not a validated or recommended tool for pain evaluation in neonates.", "answer_choice": "B"}
